title: Twist-Parametrized Points on Modular Curves
abstract:
An important open problem is to classify rational points on all modular curves, or equivalently to classify the possible adelic Galois images of elliptic curves over Q, as envisioned in Mazur’s Program B. However, this problem is inherently infinite in nature: there are infinitely many modular curves with rational points, including infinitely many with finitely many points and infinitely many with infinitely many points.
Building on Zywina’s notion of agreeable subgroups, we introduce the concepts of twist-parametrized and twist-isolated points on modular curves. Assuming standard conjectures on images of Galois representations of elliptic curves over Q, we show that all non-cuspidal, non-CM rational points on arbitrary modular curves are "twist-parametrized" by the rational points on 160 explicit modular curves.
This is joint work with Maarten Derickx, Sachi Hashimoto and Ari Shnidman.

proof_stages.label: Adelic Galois representations of elliptic curves
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a non-CM elliptic curve over Q, reviews its mod-n, l-adic, and full adelic Galois representations, and denotes the adelic image by G_E.

proof_stages.label: Serre open image and uniformity backdrop
proof_stages.summary: Serre's open image theorem gives finite-index adelic image and curve-dependent large-prime surjectivity, while Serre/Sutherland uniformity predicts a uniform bound, essentially 13 apart from known exceptional j-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Modular-curve reformulation of Mazur's program
proof_stages.summary: Modular curves X_G are introduced as moduli of elliptic curves with prescribed level-structure quotient, making the classification of possible images G_E equivalent to rational-points problems, but unbounded genus and infinitely many relevant modular curves force a further organizing principle.

proof_stages.label: Generic maximality and the adelic obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that mod-l images are generically surjective whereas adelic images are never fully surjective and are generically index two, so the classification must separate unavoidable abelian determinant/discriminant data from the nonabelian core.

proof_stages.label: Twist families from abelian modular covers
proof_stages.summary: A motivating construction shows that points on a lower modular curve can lift to rational points on abelian twists of a higher modular curve, producing infinitely many high-genus modular curves with points while still being parametrized by a base family.

proof_stages.label: Agreeable closures as universal abelian quotients
proof_stages.summary: Using Zywina's agreeable closure g_ag of an open subgroup g, the map X_g to X_{g_ag} becomes the universal modular abelian quotient with the same commutator, and toric operators encode the relevant twisting symmetries.

proof_stages.label: Twist-parametrized and twist-isolated points
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines when one modular curve, and hence a rational j-invariant, is twist-parametrized over another via modular abelian maps and rational points on twists; objects not lying in such twist families are called twist-isolated.

proof_stages.label: Finite conditional classification by twist data
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the strong Serre uniformity conjecture and Zywina's conjecture on rational points of agreeable curves, finitely many agreeable bases remain, leading to a minimal list of 160 twist families with character data that parameterize every non-CM adelic image over Q.

examples.name: Mazur torsion as a modular-curve rational-points problem
examples.note: Mazur's torsion theorem is used as a concrete illustration that prescribing visible torsion is a rational-points question on curves such as X_1(m,n), a shadow of the fuller adelic-image problem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker states Mazur’s torsion theorem and reframes it as determining rational points on modular curves like X_1(m,n), but does not carry out the determination or computations in the example.

passes.reason: Mazur torsion is presented as a standard application of rational points on modular curves, not as a structural anomaly or failure case.
Mazur torsion is cited as a standard modular-curve reformulation/application, not as a structurally special object or refutation.

It is cited as a standard application translating Mazur torsion into rational points on modular curves, not as a disproof or structurally sporadic object.
It is a standard illustrative recasting of Mazur’s torsion classification as rational points on modular curves, with no single structurally special object singled out.
used as a standard positive application of modular curves to torsion, not as an intrinsically special object or a refutation.

examples.name: Random polynomial with Galois group S_n
examples.note: The speaker compares generic adelic maximality for elliptic curves with the familiar fact that a random degree-n polynomial generically has full symmetric Galois group, purely as an analogy.
examples.engagement_reason: The random degree-n polynomial with Galois group S_n is used only as an analogy for generic largeness of images, with no concrete polynomial analyzed.

passes.reason: A random degree-n polynomial with Galois group S_n is used as the generic typical behavior analogy, not as a counterexample.
A random degree-n polynomial with Galois group S_n is presented as the generic 100% case and only as an analogy.
A degree-n polynomial with Galois group S_n has the full largest possible permutation Galois group and is used as a typical analogy, not against a claim.
A degree-n polynomial with Galois group S_n has the full maximal possible Galois group and is used as a generic analogy, not as a counterexample.
a random polynomial with Galois group S_n is presented as the density-one generic case rather than as a special example
An S_n Galois group realizes the largest possible permutation action for a degree-n polynomial, used only as a generic analogy.
The random polynomial is used as a generic analogy, while Galois group S_n is the full maximal symmetric permutation group.
It is used as the generic 100% comparison case for degree-n polynomials, not as a counterexample or atypical structure.
It is used as a generic analogy, and having Galois group S_n gives the full maximal root-permutation group for degree n.
a polynomial with Galois group S_n has the full/maximal possible permutation action on its roots and is used only as a generic analogy.
Invoked as a generic analogy, with full S_n giving the maximal symmetric Galois action.
The polynomial is used as a typical analogy, and its Galois group S_n is the full/maximal possible permutation group on n roots.

examples.name: 25-isogeny lifts to twists of X_1(125)
examples.note: The family of elliptic curves with a 25-isogeny is presented as revealing the organizing principle: rational points on a lower modular curve lift to rational points only on suitable abelian/cyclotomic twists of a higher modular curve, motivating twist-parametrized points.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the 25-isogeny case through the construction, compares the expected B_0(25) image with the actual smaller image, explains the lift to twists of X_1(125), and computes/discusses genera including 12 and 37.

passes.reason: The 25-isogeny case uses the genus-zero curve X_0(25) as a minimal-genus setting and shows the naive expected adelic image B_0(25) fails via lifts to twists of X_1(125).
The 25-isogeny condition uses the genus-zero base X_0(25)≅P^1, and the lift to twists shows the naive B_0(25)/finite-list expectation fails.
The 25-isogeny example is used to defeat the naive expectation that the adelic image should be as large as the prescribed B_0(25) level structure.
The 25-isogeny example is used to show the naive expectation that the adelic image is simply B_0(25) fails because points lift to twists of X_1(125).
the genus-zero X0(25) isogeny locus is used to show the naive expected adelic image/genus fails via lifts to twists of X1(125)
The 25-isogeny condition comes from the genus-zero curve X0(25), and the lift to twists refutes the naive maximal-image expectation.
The 25-isogeny lifting example is used to defeat the naive expectation of the largest prescribed adelic image, but it is a systematic twist-family phenomenon.
It refutes the naive expectation that a 25-isogeny gives the full prescribed Borel image, and it starts from the genus-zero curve X0(25)≅P1.
It is used to show the naive expectation of full B_0(25)-type adelic image fails, while the bottom modular curve X_0(25) is genus zero, the minimal-genus boundary case.
the 25-isogeny case starts from the genus-zero curve X_0(25) and is used to defeat the naive expectation that the adelic image is just the prescribed Borel.
Used to refute the naive B0(25) image expectation via the genus-zero boundary curve X0(25)≅P1.
The 25-isogeny example obstructs the naive expectation that the image is just B_0(25), with the genus-zero X_0(25) base lifting to twists of X_1(125).
